title: Lesson 350
transcript: We're talking about network security, of course, and one of the most important things that you need to think about in network security is have an inventory, for example, of all of your different connections that you have from your network to untrusted networks out there and just the nature of how we've evolved in networks and so on is that typically we are connected to all kinds of different architectures out there. Not only the internet or the web to allow our clients and customers to be able to interface with us and order our products, for example, and so on. But you may have connections to other third parties, including vendors and consultants and others that you have relationships with. And so, if you remember back to when we talked about physical security, we said the best way to protect anything is to ensure that you only have one entrance and one exit, for example, so that you can monitor who's coming in and who's leaving. The same concept applies in network security. We only want one entrance and one exit so that we can monitor everything that's going in and everything that's leaving so that we can enforce really good rules. But the way that, again, we've evolved in network security is that we may need a whole bunch of different connections to third parties that we have relationships with. And so part of network security needs to really understand those connections that you do have with third parties and secure them by using obviously firewalls and intrusion detection systems and have the ability to respond you know, if there are problems, for example. So, you know, this is really a summary of what third party connectivity means from a security perspective, specifically network security. So for example, know exactly the connections that you have, maintain an inventory of those connections and apply firewall rules to be able to protect each one of them, but also protect based on the services and the type of traffic, for example, that's going in and out and have proper policies to be able to dictate, for example, the rules that you're going to apply at those connections that you have. And then of course, There are security standards that exist out there that can guide organizations in how to secure connections with third parties that are providing, for example, services to you that may include cloud and others. One of the most important things that we need to do at those connections is to monitor in real time so that we can react using our incident management capabilities very quickly.
